summary: Gruber dissects the widespread credulous reporting of a debunked Strategy Analytics claim that Samsung had surpassed Apple in phone profits. He catalogs the many major publications that ran the story without correction, while noting PC Mag's David Murphy as a rare exception who followed up. Gruber argues the word 'finally' in Murphy's original headline reveals the underlying bias: a preconceived narrative that Apple's dominance is an aberration destined to end. He extends the critique to the media's reflexive trust in 'analysts' regardless of track record or methodology. Gruber acknowledges his own susceptibility to confirmation bias, describing his practice of waiting before linking stories that confirm his priors. He concludes that the correct journalistic mindset is not infallibility but rapid self-correction.
key_points: Numerous major publications reported Strategy Analytics' debunked Samsung-beats-Apple profit claim without issuing corrections
The word 'finally' in PC Mag's headline reveals a preconceived narrative that Apple's market dominance is an aberration destined to end
Media outlets grant unearned credibility to anyone labeled an 'analyst' regardless of track record or methodology
Gruber admits his own vulnerability to confirmation bias and describes deliberately delaying links to stories that reinforce his priors
The correct journalistic standard is not perpetual correctness but willingness to correct errors quickly and openly
money_quotes: It's a pre-conceived narrative driving the facts, rather than the facts driving the narrative.
Another factor is the strange affliction that causes many to believe whatever is attributed to an 'analyst', no matter the analyst's track record.
Some analysts are truly insightful; others are flat-out morons.
The right way to do this is to draw the narrative from the facts. The wrong way — but easy way — is to force the facts to fit a preconceived narrative.
I'd rather be late to link and right than early to link and wrong.
'Be right all the time' is a worthy goal but impossible; 'Try to be right all the time, but when wrong, get right as soon as you can' is the correct mindset.
I worry endlessly about falling into this trap myself.
key_insight: When media outlets share a preconceived narrative — in this case, Apple's inevitable decline — they'll credulously amplify dubious data that confirms it while ignoring evidence that debunks it.
